episode_title: Neil Brown: The Pulitzer Prizes, AI Transparency, and Journalism's Next Evolution

codes.description: Adding AI disclosure to the Pulitzer questionnaire framed not as policing but as extending journalism's pre-existing commitment to transparency
excerpts: We didn't say AI was disqualifying in any fashion, and as you can tell from the two that won, it wasn't. But we also didn't linger on essentially a moral judgment about it. We just really believed that transparency is a value we should live by.
if you used AI, you, the news organization, or the particular journalists involved, you know, employed the tools of AI. We'd like to know about it. Did you use it? And what can you explain about it?
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Recognizing that past tools like satellite imagery and CAR were never subjected to the same disclosure requirements, suggesting AI is receiving disproportionate scrutiny
excerpts: we also kind of acknowledged that we've never asked about a number of other tools like other technological tools, other computer assisted reporting. Tools we never asked to see the software or what kind of techniques went into data visualization which has been honored.

we've created a conversation point that sounds like something is brand new. But it may simply reflect that we're trying to keep up with tools and that maybe we've thrown a little too much pressure on did you use AI when we didn't ask some of these questions before.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: The Pulitzer's AI disclosures may not represent the first AI use in prize-winning journalism -- only the first time the question was asked
excerpts: We don't really know if that was the first to use them or the first to be awarded. We don't know because we just never asked the question before.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Anchoring the AI ethics conversation in longstanding journalistic norms against misrepresentation, making AI disclosure feel less novel
excerpts: Deception is bad. You should not be deceiving people how you've got the information. You should not be deceiving your audience. We don't endorse going undercover or misidentifying or misrepresentation.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Pulitzer evaluation focuses on the complete journalism produced rather than any single component, including AI use
excerpts: it's entirely about the journalism they produced. All up and down the line. Any one component was not the defining point. It's always about the totality of the work.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Frustration with the simplistic good-or-bad framing of AI in journalism, calling for context-dependent evaluation
excerpts: I still think sometimes the discussion around AI can be quite binary. It's a good or a bad.
quit making me pick it's one or the other, or one is better than the other. It's going to depend on what's the story and what's the form.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown / Nikita Roy

codes.description: Explicit refusal to pit human creativity against technological capability, positioning them as complementary
excerpts: I want us to not put creativity and technology at war with each other.
I don't like the idea of saying no, if it's, you know, machines can't write. I don't know.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: AI-generated content unlikely to meet Pulitzer standards if it lacks the distinctive quality that defines great journalism
excerpts: if you think of a machine or technology as distilling things as effectively as they may do into something that is not ultimately distinctive or distinguished, then it's probably not going to make the cut.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Using BuzzFeed/NYT's Uyghur investigation as evidence that technology-driven journalism long predates the generative AI conversation
excerpts: they used satellite imageries and computer technology to try to map out where they likely were
before everybody was using the phrase generator of AI, and it might not have been what is technically AI at all. The technology was advancing the journalism effort enormously.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: AI positioned as the latest step in a decades-long evolution of technological tools in journalism, not a rupture
excerpts: technology has been advancing the cause of journalism for a good long while. And tools change, ideas change.
I think technology has been bolstering and improving journalism for several decades. This is the latest use of it.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Whether AI meets journalistic standards depends on the specific mission of the work -- creative writing vs. information gathering
excerpts: If the mission of the work is a creative endeavor or enterprise, then maybe a large language model is not gonna meet the creative skill. If it's a gathering of certain information in unbelievable speed is the mission, well then maybe it's going to compete, it's going to be distinctive and distinguished.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Both AI tools and human reporters make errors; technology neither erases nor automatically causes inaccuracy
excerpts: Technology does not erase inaccuracy, much as it doesn't automatically cause it either. So you have to you've got to embrace the reality in the gray area that no matter how powerful the tools are, imperfection is part of the challenge.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Repeated insistence that audience value should drive AI adoption decisions, not internal convenience or novelty
excerpts: put the audience first, then you can go from there.
what serves the audience when in fact maybe the most simple and direct thing serves the audience
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Drawing analogy between AI provenance concerns and longstanding problems with anonymous sourcing to show enduring principles
excerpts: anonymous sources. So we teach the challenges of or the problem that whether it's a person or it's scraped by a tool, that and you give people information but they don't know where it comes from and they can't make a judgment about the credibility of the source, that's a problem in either direction.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Calling for cultural transformation where newsrooms integrate technologists and journalists rather than siloing them
excerpts: newsrooms today, in my opinion, need to be a much more comfortable blend of technologists and journalists. And by the way, technologists need to know a fair bit about journalism and journalists need to know a fair bit about the technology.
I've had the best luck when the technologists first ask what are you trying to do? Not do I have the tool for it.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Identifying organizational culture, not technology, as the primary barrier to successful AI integration
excerpts: some of what we're teaching is just the culture of a new environment.
we need to get past sort of a an artificial analysis that says anything you're doing today if any technology is a threat to that, the technology must be bad.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: The print-to-digital transition's biggest mistake was ignoring change; AI adoption must not repeat this pattern
excerpts: The number one mistake to avoid doing again is to put your head down and hope it goes away. I do think that there was a little bit of that, maybe not a little bit, maybe a lot of that, as we moved to online digital journalism.
print throws off most of the revenues still, you know, and sure, but all you're doing is avoiding
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Alarm that in 2025, majority of some newsrooms have never tried basic AI tools like ChatGPT
excerpts: it's 2025 and I'm still getting into newsrooms where over 60% of the newsroom has never even tried out a tool like ChatGPT or even understanding the potential of just what's at risk if we don't evolve.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Framing the refusal to try AI tools before judging them as bad journalism -- failure to investigate before opining
excerpts: you can't have an opinion about a tool that you don't use or have experimented with. It's bad reporting. It's bad journalism to do that.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Personal experience with ChatGPT revealing it as simultaneously impressive and insufficient, reinforcing the value of human editing
excerpts: it's amazingly sophisticated, cool, and weak all at the same time. Right? Because it's not, well, that's not good enough. That answer is just simply not good enough.
some of these programs remind me, you need an editor. And they've reinforced my value, not spooked me.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: AI's limitations actually reinforce the irreplaceable value of editorial judgment and human editing
excerpts: some of these programs remind me, you need an editor. And they've reinforced my value, not spooked me. Even as I've been impressed by how fast they are.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Advocating for repurposing existing workforce skills rather than framing AI as pure job destruction
excerpts: if there are things you did very well before that remain valuable, even as the form changes, we can explore how to keep the value of those fundamentals but retrain and repurpose them toward a more modern set of experiences and uses
you didn't have before? Multi-platform editors. So people get retrained, they adapt, their skills change
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Reframing job displacement anxiety by asking what the organization needs to achieve rather than what jobs need protecting
excerpts: they're starting from the standpoint of we don't want the work to change or we have to protect the job as opposed to what is the goal a year from now or two years from now. Start with the end in mind.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Framing the current moment as a third infrastructure shift: from print to digital to AI, with tech companies as new middlemen
excerpts: It was a print infrastructure, then you had a digital infrastructure. We are now having an AI infrastructure, which is going to be changing and tech companies are becoming the middlemen in that layer.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Criticizing short evaluation cycles for AI experiments and advocating sustained commitment to learning
excerpts: the commercial connection with people, it's a long game. And if they don't suddenly love your product tomorrow, that doesn't mean they won't love it down the road
we tried that for three months, six months, didn't work. First of all, odds are they didn't really try it. Or they tried it only internally.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Urging newsrooms to involve audiences in AI product development from the start, not after deployment
excerpts: how about engaging your audience at this stage? Not just about how you can serve them once you figured out what the tool can do, but what if you brought in audience members now
Let's drive down the road together rather than wait till they get there and then they have to wait for us to catch up.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Need to reduce fear and mystification around AI tools through direct hands-on experience
excerpts: It really important to demystify, bring these fears down, not have everything be a white knuckle situation. Because we'll just it won't get us anywhere.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Journalism industry fails to adequately investigate and report on itself and its own transformation
excerpts: the journalism field made any, has sort of struggled with something, it doesn't do enough reporting on itself. And I don't mean reporting on itself like writing about the troubles at one news organization or another.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: The greatest danger to journalism is not AI itself but failing to evolve while tech companies reshape information distribution
excerpts: The biggest existential threat that's there to journalism is if we don't evolve, because you're seeing tech companies constantly evolving the way in which people are just getting information.
codes.speaker: Nikita Roy

codes.description: Institutional effort to create and update AI ethics guidelines while encouraging experimentation
excerpts: We just put out our AI ethics guidelines, updated them. We've had them for a while. And we're encouraging people to not be afraid of using, say, ChatGPT, Perplexity, Fireflies
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

codes.description: Closing philosophical frame positioning journalism's future as dependent on creative vision rather than defensive posture
excerpts: to me it's about imagination and possibilities and that's that's where the business needs to go.
codes.speaker: Neil Brown

episode_summary: This episode reveals a senior journalism leader carefully threading the needle between institutional conservatism and technological openness. Brown's most analytically significant move is historicizing AI within a longer continuum of technological tools in journalism -- from satellite imagery to CAR to generative AI -- which simultaneously normalizes AI adoption and exposes the inconsistency of applying disclosure requirements only to AI when prior tools were never scrutinized. His repeated refusal to frame AI as binary (good/bad, creative/mechanical) suggests the journalism establishment is moving toward a contextual, mission-dependent evaluation framework rather than blanket rules. The conversation surfaces a critical institutional gap: despite Brown's emphasis on experimentation, Nikita Roy's statistic that over 60% of newsroom staff have never tried ChatGPT reveals how far practice lags behind rhetoric. Perhaps most revealing is Brown's personal assessment of ChatGPT as 'sophisticated, cool, and weak all at the same time' -- a characterization that reinforces editorial authority rather than threatening it, suggesting that senior journalism leaders may find AI less disruptive to their professional identity than feared.
surprises: The Pulitzer Board cannot confirm whether the 2024 winners were actually the first to use AI -- only the first to be asked about it, suggesting AI use in award-winning journalism may have a longer unacknowledged history
Brown's self-characterization as using ChatGPT 'mostly as a party game' for drafting remarks suggests even pro-AI institutional leaders have shallow personal engagement with the tools
The parallel drawn between AI disclosure and the decades-old anonymous sources problem frames a cutting-edge debate in surprisingly traditional journalistic terms

Despite leading a journalism training institution, Brown's most specific AI adoption advice is simply to 'play around with ChatGPT' -- suggesting even educators lack structured approaches to AI upskilling
tensions: Brown champions transparency and disclosure about AI but acknowledges this standard was never applied to previous technological tools, creating an asymmetric burden on AI
He refuses to make moral judgments about AI in journalism while simultaneously insisting on 'distinctiveness' as a quality standard that implicitly favors human creativity
The emphasis on retraining and adaptation coexists with a candid admission that journalism's labor challenges are 'really difficult and tricky' -- optimistic framing with pessimistic undercurrents

Brown advocates longitudinal AI experimentation but the Poynter Institute's own approach appears to be primarily about encouraging individual 'dabbling' rather than systematic organizational transformation
The conversation frames journalists as needing to be 'good reporters about AI' while simultaneously revealing that over 60% have never tried the tools they would need to report on
